questions.question: Explain the difference between Protection and Security in operating systems with examples.
keyPoints: Protection controls access among processes within the system
Security guards against external threats and unauthorized access
Protection is internal; Security is external
Example: Protection prevents one user process from accessing another's memory; Security prevents hackers from gaining remote access

questions.question: Describe the Access Matrix model of protection and explain how it is implemented using Access Control Lists (ACLs).
keyPoints: Access Matrix has subjects as rows and objects as columns
Cells contain access rights like read, write, execute
ACLs are per-object lists specifying subjects and their permissions
ACLs provide fine-grained control over specific resources

questions.question: What is the Bell-LaPadula security model? Explain its two main properties with examples.
keyPoints: Focuses on confidentiality of data
Simple Security Property: No read up - cannot read higher level data
Star Property: No write down - cannot write to lower level data
Prevents information flow from higher to lower security levels

questions.question: Differentiate between Discretionary Access Control (DAC) and Mandatory Access Control (MAC).
keyPoints: DAC: Owner decides who can access their resources
MAC: System enforces policies based on security labels; users cannot override
DAC is flexible but less secure; MAC is rigid but more secure
Examples: DAC in Unix file permissions; MAC in military classification systems

questions.question: Explain Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) and its advantages over traditional access control methods.
keyPoints: Permissions assigned to roles, not individual users
Users assigned to roles based on job responsibilities
Advantages: Simplified administration, easier to manage permissions, supports least privilege
Reduces complexity when many users share similar job functions

questions.question: What is the concept of a Trusted Operating System? List its key security features.
keyPoints: Provides secure computing environment
Implements security kernel with reference monitor
Supports authentication, authorization, and auditing
Uses isolation, access control, and verification mechanisms
Meets specific security criteria (e.g., Orange Book levels)

questions.question: Explain the principle of Least Privilege and its importance in OS security.
keyPoints: Users and processes should have minimum privileges needed
Limits potential damage from accidents or security breaches
Reduces attack surface of the system
Simplifies security management and reduces risk

questions.question: Describe the Biba Integrity Model. How does it differ from the Bell-LaPadula model in terms of goals and rules? Explain with examples.
keyPoints: Biba focuses on data integrity, not confidentiality
Simple Integrity Property: No read down - prevents contamination
Star Integrity Property: No write up - prevents corruption of higher integrity data
Differs from Bell-LaPadula: Bell-LaPadula prevents data leakage (confidentiality), Biba prevents data corruption (integrity)
Example: Prevents a virus-infected low-level file from corrupting critical system files

questions.question: Explain the Reference Monitor concept in operating system security. What are the requirements for a Reference Monitor to be considered trustworthy? How is it implemented in practice?
keyPoints: Reference Monitor mediates all access between subjects and objects
Must be tamper-proof: Cannot be bypassed or modified
Must be always invoked: Called for every access attempt
Must be verifiable: Simple enough to be analyzed and tested
Implemented as Security Kernel in trusted operating systems
Provides complete mediation, isolation, and verifiability

questions.question: Discuss the various mechanisms used for user authentication in operating systems. Evaluate the strengths and weaknesses of each method.
keyPoints: Knowledge-based: Passwords - simple but vulnerable to guessing/theft
Possession-based: Smart cards/tokens - requires physical item, can be lost
Biometric-based: Fingerprint/face - unique but expensive and privacy concerns
Multi-factor: Combines two or more methods for better security
Trade-offs: Convenience vs security, cost vs protection level
Best practice: Use multi-factor authentication for critical systems

questions.question: Compare Access Control Lists (ACLs) and Capabilities as mechanisms for implementing protection in operating systems. Discuss their advantages and disadvantages.
keyPoints: ACLs: Attached to objects, list subjects and their permissions
Capabilities: Held by subjects, specify rights over objects
ACL advantages: Easy to see who can access an object
ACL disadvantages: Hard to manage for many objects/users
Capability advantages: Easy to transfer, good for distributed systems
Capability disadvantages: Hard to revoke, risk if copied/stolen
Trade-off: Revocation is easier with ACLs; Delegation is easier with capabilities
